source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells

extracts.extract: Often the data you have will prompt questions that it doesn't immediately answer. The essence of mutate is using the data you have to answer a new question — calculating new metrics in a new field using existing fields of data.
extracts.notes: R/dplyr context but principle is tool-agnostic

extracts.extract: There's a mix of styles: 'Baltimore' and 'BALTIMORE' for example. R will think those are two different cities, and that will mean that any aggregates we create based on city won't be accurate. Use str_to_upper to standardize case before grouping.
extracts.notes: R-specific syntax but universal cleaning principle

extracts.extract: You should never assume that missing values won't appear in a dataset. When classifying data with case_when or if_else, always account for blank or NA values as a separate category rather than letting them fall silently into another group.

One of the most frustrating things in data is working with dates. Everyone has a different opinion on how to record them, and every software package on the planet has to sort it out. Consider using a dedicated date library (lubridate) to solve common problems before they start.
extracts.notes: R context; equivalent libraries exist in Python (pandas/dateutil)
